Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1 lists CBFD2 as Genbank: AY644758.1, and Experimental procedures lists CBFD2 cDNAs as AY644759.1 and AY644758.1.

Source_Species.reason: The Summary and Results identify CBFD2 as a plant gene from Adonis aestivalis.

Applied_Species.reason: CBFD2 was tested in transient assays in Nicotiana benthamiana and in stable transgenic camelina.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Nicotiana benthamiana and Camelina sativa are explicitly stated.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Experimental procedures specify Camelina sativa (L. cv. Suneson).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results state transient introduction into N. benthamiana leaves, and RT-PCR confirmed expression of CBFD2 in developing pASTA camelina seeds.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper states chloroplast endogenous β-carotene was used and TP was added only to bacterial crtW/crtZ, not explicitly stating CBFD2 localization, though plastid localization is plausible from pathway context.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: CBFD2 is explicitly named carotenoid β-ring 4-dehydrogenase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 and text explicitly give 'carotenoid β-ring 4-dehydrogenase 2'.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CBFD2 is presented as part of the Adonis astaxanthin/ketocarotenoid biosynthetic pathway, which is within carotenoid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper compares the Adonis plant-derived astaxanthin pathway converting β-carotene to ketocarotenoids including astaxanthin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: CBFD2 functions in the astaxanthin-forming portion of the pathway, but the exact designation 'downstream, terminal ketocarotenoid-forming steps' is not explicitly stated.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper suggests enhanced conversion of adonirubin to astaxanthin by CBFD2, but does not directly define CBFD2 substrate specificity as 'especially adonirubin' in the main pathway description.

Primary_Product.reason: CBFD2 is part of the Adonis pathway used to produce astaxanthin, and COMBI discussion specifically suggests CBFD2 enhanced conversion of adonirubin to astaxanthin.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The enzyme class as β-ring 4-dehydrogenase is explicit, and the paper suggests enhanced conversion of adonirubin to astaxanthin by CBFD2, but a full mechanistic description is not directly demonstrated experimentally here.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CBFD2 is directly part of the introduced astaxanthin pathway and linked to astaxanthin formation in leaves and seeds.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not identify CBFD2 as a rate-limiting or key control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Astaxanthin is the target end product throughout the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Astaxanthin is explicitly described as a ketocarotenoid/carotenoid pigment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Summary states astaxanthin is a natural red pigment with exceptional antioxidant capacity and value for aquaculture feed and food applications.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Astaxanthin accumulated in infiltrated leaves and in camelina seeds; seed oil extracted from ketocarotenoid-accumulating seeds was also analyzed.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show ASTA (CBFD2+HBFD1) gave a higher astaxanthin proportion and fewer intermediates than crtWZ; camelina seeds accumulated primarily astaxanthin among free ketocarotenoids.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The paper used transient N. benthamiana assays with TLC/HPLC-DAD, stable camelina transformation, RT-PCR in seeds, metabolite profiling, and field evaluation.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These conditions are described in Results and Experimental procedures.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: CBFD2 was co-expressed with HBFD1 and ZmPSY in constructs, but direct protein-protein interaction was not tested.

Applied_Promoters.reason: 35S promoter was used in transient assays; glycinin promoter was used in camelina pASTA.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: These phenotypes were observed in pASTA lines containing CBFD2/HBFD1/ZmPSY, though not attributable to CBFD2 alone.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Results/Discussion support that the plant-derived pathway was preferable to bacterial genes for plant astaxanthin engineering and was used successfully in oilseed camelina.

Other_Important_Info.reason: CBFD2 was part of ASTA; ASTA performed similarly to COMBI and better than crtWZ for astaxanthin proportion/purity in leaves.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary accurately reflects results for CBFD2 as part of the ASTA module in leaves and camelina, with higher astaxanthin proportion than bacterial crtW/crtZ and near-complete β-carotene conversion in camelina.

corrections.old_value: Key control step suggested for efficient late conversion to astaxanthin in plant host
corrections.reason: The paper does not identify CBFD2 as a rate-limiting or key control step.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1 and Experimental procedures list HBFD1 as Genbank DQ902555.1.

Source_Species.reason: HBFD1 is explicitly from Adonis aestivalis.

Applied_Species.reason: HBFD1 was tested in N. benthamiana transient assays and in camelina transgenics.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Nicotiana benthamiana and Camelina sativa are explicitly named.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Camelina sativa (L. cv. Suneson) is specified.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: HBFD1 was transiently introduced into N. benthamiana leaves and RT-PCR confirmed expression in developing pASTA camelina seeds.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper does not explicitly state HBFD1 plastid/chloroplast localization, though it functions in plastid carotenoid metabolism.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 gives 'carotenoid 4-hydroxy-β-ring 4-dehydrogenase' and Experimental procedures specify HBFD1.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: HBFD1 is part of the Adonis astaxanthin/ketocarotenoid pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: It is presented in the plant-derived route from β-carotene to astaxanthin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper places HBFD1 in astaxanthin biosynthesis but does not explicitly label it as a terminal/downstream step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: General pathway relevance is clear, but exact substrate wording is not directly specified in the paper.

Primary_Product.reason: HBFD1 contributes to astaxanthin pathway output, but the paper does not individually assign HBFD1 specific products such as astaxanthin/adonirubin/adonixanthin.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The enzyme name itself supports oxidation/dehydrogenation of a 4-hydroxy-β-ring-containing carotenoid, although detailed substrate testing was not performed here.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: HBFD1 is directly part of the introduced pathway yielding astaxanthin.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports HBFD1 as an important pathway component, but does not assess whether it is rate-limiting.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Astaxanthin is the target terminal metabolite.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Astaxanthin is described as an antioxidant red pigment useful for aquaculture and food.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Ketocarotenoids including astaxanthin accumulated in infiltrated leaves and camelina seeds/oil.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: As part of ASTA with CBFD2, HBFD1 contributed to efficient astaxanthin production with fewer intermediates than the bacterial pathway.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Methods included transient expression with TLC/HPLC-DAD and stable camelina with RT-PCR and carotenoid profiling in greenhouse and field.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: HBFD1 was co-expressed with CBFD2 and ZmPSY, but no direct physical interactions were tested.

Applied_Promoters.reason: 35S promoter for transient assays and glycinin promoter for camelina are explicit.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: These tradeoffs were observed for pASTA lines carrying HBFD1 with the other transgenes, not for HBFD1 alone.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper supports the plant-derived pathway genes as useful for engineering astaxanthin-rich oilseeds with improved performance relative to bacterial genes.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper tested HBFD1 only as part of ASTA with CBFD2; individual contributions were not separated.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary is consistent with the Results, while recognizing HBFD1 was evaluated together with CBFD2 and ZmPSY rather than alone.

Source_Species.reason: The paper states the PSY gene is from maize.

Applied_Species.reason: ZmPSY was introduced into camelina seeds in pASTA.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: RT-PCR confirmed expression of ZmPSY in developing pASTA camelina seeds.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: PSY is a plastid carotenoid enzyme by known biology, but the paper does not explicitly state localization for ZmPSY.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 and text explicitly state phytoene synthase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper states ZmPSY was included to enhance flux in the plastid isoprenoid pathway for β-carotene biosynthesis, i.e., carotenoid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: ZmPSY is described as increasing flux into the β-carotene/carotenoid pathway from plastid isoprenoid metabolism.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: As phytoene synthase, it functions upstream to enhance pathway flux; the paper explicitly uses it to increase β-carotene biosynthesis.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper mentions GGPP in the pathway figure and discusses GGPP as a precursor, but does not explicitly state ZmPSY substrate in the Results text.

Primary_Product.reason: As PSY the product phytoene is standard biochemistry, but the paper itself does not explicitly state phytoene as the product.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The flux-enhancing role in carotenoid biosynthesis is supported, but the exact reaction 'condenses two molecules of GGPP to form phytoene' is not explicitly described in the paper.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: ZmPSY was included to enhance precursor supply for β-carotene and downstream ketocarotenoid/astaxanthin production.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not identify ZmPSY as a major flux-control or rate-limiting step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Astaxanthin is the engineered end product in camelina.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Astaxanthin is described as a natural antioxidant red pigment useful in aquaculture and food.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Ketocarotenoids accumulated in camelina seeds and oil.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper explicitly says ZmPSY was included because camelina seeds have low β-carotene, to enhance flux for β-carotene biosynthesis; pASTA seeds showed high ketocarotenoid accumulation.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Stable transgenic camelina, RT-PCR, greenhouse and field carotenoid profiling were used.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Seed-specific expression in greenhouse and field conditions is described.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: ZmPSY was co-expressed with CBFD2 and HBFD1, but no direct interaction was tested.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The pASTA vector carried all three transgenes under a seed-specific glycinin promoter.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Reduced tocopherol and oil content in pASTA are consistent with altered flux, but these effects cannot be assigned specifically to ZmPSY from the paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper supports using ZmPSY as a precursor-supply gene to improve astaxanthin engineering in camelina.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states camelina seeds have relatively low β-carotene concentrations, motivating inclusion of ZmPSY.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary matches the paper's treatment of ZmPSY as a precursor-supply transgene in the pASTA construct that supported high ketocarotenoid accumulation.

corrections.reason: No EC number is provided in the paper.

corrections.old_value: Committed-step enzyme and major flux-control point for carotenoid accumulation
corrections.reason: The paper does not identify ZmPSY as a major flux-control or rate-limiting step.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1 and Experimental procedures list crtW as Genbank QVQ68840.1.

Source_Species.reason: The paper states the bacterial genes are from Brevundimonas sp.

Applied_Species.reason: crtW was tested by transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: crtW was transiently expressed in infiltrated leaves as TP-crtW.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The coding sequence included an N-terminal plastid transit peptide for plastid targeting.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: crtW is explicitly identified as β-carotene ketolase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 gives β-carotene ketolase for crtW.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: crtW is part of the bacterial astaxanthin/ketocarotenoid biosynthesis pathway tested in leaves.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper contrasts the bacterial crtW/crtZ route with the Adonis plant-derived pathway to astaxanthin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: crtW performs ketolation in the downstream astaxanthin-forming portion of the pathway; this is consistent with the enzyme identity presented.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper does not directly specify crtW substrates in this study, though β-carotene-derived carotenoids are implied.

Primary_Product.reason: crtWZ-infiltrated leaves accumulated canthaxanthin, adonirubin and other ketocarotenoid intermediates, consistent with crtW activity.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: As β-carotene ketolase, crtW introduces keto groups onto β-ionone rings; this is directly supported by the enzyme name and pathway context.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: crtW contributes to ketocarotenoid intermediates leading to astaxanthin in the bacterial pathway.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not establish crtW as rate-limiting and overall shows the bacterial pathway was less efficient; this wording is consistent with the evidence.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Astaxanthin is the end product measured in crtWZ-infiltrated leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper describes astaxanthin as an antioxidant red pigment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Astaxanthin accumulated in N. benthamiana leaves after infiltration.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: crtWZ supported ketocarotenoid formation but accumulated more intermediates and had lower astaxanthin proportion than ASTA.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Transient expression with TLC and HPLC-DAD profiling in N. benthamiana leaves was used.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Agrobacterium infiltration and 5 DAI analysis are explicitly described.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: crtW was co-expressed with crtZ, but direct protein interaction was not tested.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The transient constructs were flanked by the CaMV35S promoter and 3' UTR.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly show accumulation of intermediates such as adonirubin and canthaxanthin in crtWZ leaves, indicating incomplete conversion.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Results/Discussion indicate the bacterial pathway was inferior to the plant-derived pathway in this plant host under tested conditions.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly says crtW was fused to an Arabidopsis Rubisco small subunit transit peptide.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary accurately reflects the reported transient assay results for crtW as part of crtWZ.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Figure 1 and Experimental procedures list crtZ as Genbank WP_183216731.1.

Source_Species.reason: The bacterial genes including crtZ are from Brevundimonas sp.

Applied_Species.reason: crtZ was tested in transient assays in Nicotiana benthamiana leaves.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: crtZ was transiently expressed in infiltrated leaves as TP-crtZ.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: crtZ included an N-terminal plastid transit peptide for plastid targeting.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: crtZ is explicitly identified as β-carotene hydroxylase.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 gives β-carotene hydroxylase for crtZ.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: crtZ is part of the bacterial astaxanthin/ketocarotenoid pathway examined in leaves.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The bacterial crtW/crtZ route to astaxanthin is explicitly contrasted with the Adonis pathway.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: crtZ functions in downstream hydroxylation toward astaxanthin, consistent with its enzyme identity and reported intermediate conversion issue.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper specifically discusses low efficiency of conversion of adonirubin into astaxanthin by crtZ, and accumulation of canthaxanthin/adonirubin implies these ketolated intermediates are crtZ-related substrates.

Primary_Product.reason: Astaxanthin and adonixanthin were among ketocarotenoids detected in crtWZ-infiltrated leaves.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: As β-carotene hydroxylase, crtZ hydroxylates ketocarotenoid intermediates toward astaxanthin; the paper specifically discusses adonirubin-to-astaxanthin conversion by crtZ.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: crtZ catalyzes a late conversion toward astaxanthin in the bacterial pathway and this conversion was incomplete in the plant host.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not formally establish rate limitation, but explicitly suggests low efficiency of crtZ-mediated adonirubin-to-astaxanthin conversion as the cause of intermediate accumulation, so the statement is consistent.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Astaxanthin is the measured target product in crtWZ leaves.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper describes astaxanthin as an antioxidant red pigment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Astaxanthin accumulated in N. benthamiana leaves after infiltration.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results explicitly state low efficiency of adonirubin-to-astaxanthin conversion by crtZ caused accumulation of intermediates in the plant host.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Transient expression in N. benthamiana with TLC/HPLC-DAD carotenoid profiling was used.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Agrobacterium infiltration and 5 DAI assessment are explicit.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: crtZ was co-expressed with crtW, but direct interaction was not tested.

Applied_Promoters.reason: The transient vectors used the CaMV35S promoter.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Incomplete conversion and higher adonirubin/canthaxanthin accumulation are directly described for crtWZ.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The study uses crtZ as a comparator and concludes the Adonis pathway is preferable in plant hosts under tested conditions.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper specifically attributes lower astaxanthin proportion in crtWZ leaves to low efficiency of crtZ converting adonirubin into astaxanthin.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: This summary matches the Results discussing crtZ in the bacterial pathway and its apparent inefficiency in the plant host.
